volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: Curculio (Section 1.1.1 - Section 1.1.74)

sections.label: Section 1.1.1
sections.original: Quo ted hoc noctis dicam proficisci foras
sections.translation: Whither away must I say that you are going out of doors at this time o ’ night , Phaedromus , with that dress and with this train [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.3
sections.original: Quo Venus Cupidoque imperat , suadet Amor :
sections.translation: Whither Venus and Cupid summon me , and Love persuades me ; whether ’ tis the midnight or whether the earliest twilight ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.5
sections.original: si status condictus cum hoste intercedit dies ,
sections.translation: if the day is fixed [*] for pleading your cause with your antagonist , still must you go where they command you , whether you will or no .

sections.label: Section 1.1.7
sections.translation: But pray , pray —

sections.label: Section 1.1.7b
sections.translation: Pray — you are annoying to me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.8
sections.original: Istuc quidem nec bellum est nec memorabile :
sections.translation: Really that is neither pretty nor befitting you to say . You are your own servant [*] ; in your fine garb you are showing the light with your waxen torch [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.10
sections.original: Egon apicularum congestum opera non feram ,
sections.translation: And ought I not to carry that which is gathered by the labour of the little bees — which has its birth in sweets — to my own sweet one , my little honey ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.12
sections.original: Nam quo te dicam ego ire ?
sections.translation: But whither must I say that you are going ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.12b
sections.translation: If you ask me that , I ’ ll tell , so that you may know .

sections.label: Section 1.1.13b
sections.translation: If I make the enquiry , what would you answer me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.14
sections.translation: This is the Temple ( pointing to it ) of Aesculapius .

sections.label: Section 1.1.14b
sections.translation: That I have known for more than a twelvemonth past .

sections.label: Section 1.1.15
sections.translation: Close to it is that door , dear as my very eyes . ( Points to the door of CAPPADOX .) Hail to you ! door , dear as my very eyes ; have you been quite well of late ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.16b
sections.translation: Did a fever leave you [*] but yesterday , or the day before , and did you take your dinner yesterday ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.18b
sections.translation: Are you laughing at me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.19
sections.original: Quid tu ergo , insane , rogitas valeatne ostium ?
sections.translation: Why then , madman , are you enquiring whether the door is well or not ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.20
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ ve known it as a door most comely and most discreet : never one word does it whisper ; when it is opened , it is silent ; and when , by night , she secretly comes out to me , it holds its peace .

sections.label: Section 1.1.23
sections.original: Numquid tu quod te aut genere indignum sit tuo
sections.translation: And are you not , Phaedromus , doing , or contemplating the doing of , some deed which is unworthy of yourself or of your family ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.25
sections.original: num tu pudicae cuipiam insidias locas
sections.translation: Are you not laying a snare for some modest fair one , or for one that should be modest ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.26b
sections.translation: For no one ; and may Jupiter not permit me to do so .

sections.label: Section 1.1.27b
sections.translation: I wish the same . Ever , if you are wise , so bestow your love , that if the public should know the object which you love , it may be no disgrace to you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.30
sections.translation: Ever do you take care that you be not disgraced [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.32
sections.translation: What means that expression ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.32b
sections.translation: For you to proceed with caution on your path ; the object that you love , love in the presence of witnesses .

sections.label: Section 1.1.33
sections.translation: Why , ’ tis a Procurer that lives here .

sections.label: Section 1.1.33b
sections.translation: No one drives you away from there , nor yet forbids you , if you have the money , to buy what ’ s openly on sale .

sections.label: Section 1.1.35
sections.original: nemo ire quemquam publica prohibet via ;
sections.translation: No one forbids any person from going along the public road , so long as he doesn ’ t make a path through the field that ’ s fenced around ; so long as you keep yourself away from the wife , the widow , the maiden , youthful age , and free-born children , love what you please .

sections.label: Section 1.1.39
sections.translation: This is the house of a Procurer .

sections.label: Section 1.1.39b
sections.translation: A curse befall it .

sections.label: Section 1.1.40
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.40b
sections.translation: Because it serves in an infamous service .

sections.label: Section 1.1.41
sections.translation: You speak out .

sections.label: Section 1.1.41b
sections.translation: Be it so , most especially .

sections.label: Section 1.1.41c
sections.translation: Once more , will you hold your tongue ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.42
sections.translation: You bade me speak out [*] , I thought .

sections.label: Section 1.1.42b
sections.translation: Then , now I forbid you . But , as I had begun to say , he has a young female slave —

sections.label: Section 1.1.44
sections.original: Nempe huic lenoni qui hic habitat ?
sections.translation: This Procurer , you mean , who ’ s living here ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.44b
sections.translation: You have hold of it exactly .

sections.label: Section 1.1.45
sections.translation: The less shall I be in dread of its falling .

sections.label: Section 1.1.45b
sections.translation: You are impertinent . He wishes to make her a courtesan , while she is desperately in love with me ; whereas I don ’ t wish to have her upon loan .

sections.label: Section 1.1.48
sections.translation: Why so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.48b
sections.original: Quia proprium facio : amo pariter simul .
sections.translation: Because I ’ m for having her as my own ; I love her equally as well .

sections.label: Section 1.1.49
sections.original: Malus clándestinus est amor , damnumst merum .
sections.translation: Clandestine courtship is bad ; ’ tis utter ruin .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: Est hercle ita ut tu dicis .
sections.translation: I ’ troth , ’ tis so as you say .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50b
sections.translation: Has she as yet submitted to the yoke of Venus ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.51
sections.original: Tam a me pudica est quasi soror mea sit , nisi
sections.translation: For me she is as chaste as though she were my own sister , unless , indeed , she is any the more unchaste for some kissing .

sections.label: Section 1.1.53
sections.original: Semper tu scito , flamma fumo est proxima ;
sections.translation: Always , do you understand , flame follows very close on smoke ; with smoke , nothing can be burnt , with flame , it can .

sections.label: Section 1.1.55
sections.original: qui é nuce nuculeum esse volt , frangit nucem :
sections.translation: He who wishes to eat the kernel [*] of the nut , first breaks the nut ; he who wishes to seduce , opens the dance [*] with kisses .

sections.label: Section 1.1.57
sections.original: At illa ést pudica neque dum cubitat cum viris .
sections.translation: But she is chaste , and never yet has bestowed her favours upon man .

sections.label: Section 1.1.58
sections.original: Credam , pudor si cuiquam lenoni siet .
sections.translation: That I could believe [*] , if any Procurer had any shame .

sections.label: Section 1.1.59
sections.original: Immo ut illam censes ? ut quaeque illi occasiost ,
sections.translation: Well , but what think you of her ? When she has any opportunity ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.60
sections.original: subripere se ad me ; ubi savium oppegit , fugit .
sections.translation: she steals away to me ; when she has given me a kiss , she ’ s off again . This happens by reason of this , because this Procurer is lying a-bed ill in the Temple [*] of Aesculapius ; that fellow is my torturer .

sections.label: Section 1.1.62b
sections.translation: How so ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.63
sections.original: Alias me poscit pro illa triginta minas ,
sections.translation: At one time he asks me for thirty minae for her , at another for a great talent ; and from him I cannot obtain any fair and just dealing .

sections.label: Section 1.1.65b
sections.translation: You are too exacting , in requiring that of him which no Procurer possesses .

sections.label: Section 1.1.67
sections.original: Nunc hinc parasitum in Cariam misi meum
sections.translation: Now , I ’ ve sent my Parasite hence to Caria [*] , to ask for money on loan from my friend ; if he doesn ’ t bring me this , which way to turn myself I know not .

sections.label: Section 1.1.70
sections.translation: If you salute the Deities , towards the right [*] I think ; now this is this altar of Venus before their door .

sections.label: Section 1.1.71
sections.original: Nunc ara Veneris haec est ante horunc fores ;
sections.translation: I have already vowed to bring me [*] an early breakfast for my Venus [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.73
sections.original: Quid ? tu te pones Veneri ieientaculo ?
sections.translation: What ? Will you then be giving up yourself as a breakfast to Venus ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.74
sections.translation: Myself , and you , and all of these .
